description: Default permissions for the desktop window. The structure: fs:scope allow=** (broad) with deny entries for sensitive locations (credentials, browser profiles, shell history, app's own session DB). Fs scope applies only to FRONTEND-INITIATED file operations via the FS plugin. Why broad: input files commonly live on D:/E:/ drives outside $HOME; restricting to $HOME breaks multi-drive workflows. The scope governs reads + exists only — output writes (write-text-file / copy / rename) used to be plugin-fs callsites and were removed (Codex 776ff6ef / 818eb84f / d29ac141: plugin-fs follows reparse points and lets a planted symlink in a fan-sub pack redirect writes outside the picked dir). Output writes now live on Rust-side `safe_io` commands that enforce symlink rejection on source AND destination independently of fs:scope. Why safe: the user picks input files via native dialog (no path injection surface), and the load-bearing Rust backend commands enforce their own access controls independent of fs:scope: read_text_detect_encoding has an extension whitelist, subset_font checks the in-memory provenance cache, scan_font_directory canonicalizes + verifies starts_with the picked dir. Font directories are intentionally NOT in the deny list — Rust commands read fonts via std::fs::read after their own validation, not through the FS plugin. The deny list mirrors the cross-tool 'no app should ever read this' set + this app's own AppData/LocalAppData session DB. App-data-dir deny entries (com.koagaroon.ssahdrify) block JS-side reads of the app's own session SQLite + cache file; if a future debug command exposes those paths to JS, this deny entry needs revisiting. Linux/macOS deny entries cover shell history, editor state, and the GNOME/KDE keyring stores. Permission bundles: dialog:default (tauri-plugin-dialog 2.7.0) already covers allow-message + allow-save + allow-open, so we do NOT list those individually — adding them would mislead a future reader into believing dialog:default is incomplete and proliferate explicit grants under that false assumption.
